mnemonics.title: Requirements Model Components
mnemonics.content: SCFB: Scenario-based (Use Cases), Class-based (Objects), Flow-oriented (DFDs), Behavioral (State/Sequence)

mnemonics.title: Multiplicity Quick Reference
mnemonics.content: 1 = exactly one, 0..1 = optional one, * = many (0 to unlimited), 1..* = at least one, n..m = range (min to max)

mnemonics.title: CRC Card Purpose
mnemonics.content: CRC: Class (what), Responsibility (what it knows/does), Collaboration (who it talks to)

mnemonics.title: DFD Elements Memory
mnemonics.content: PEDS: Process (transforms), External Entity (source/sink), Data Store (storage), Data Flow (movement)

mnemonics.title: Requirements Validation Techniques
mnemonics.content: PRoTECT: Prototyping, Reviews, Test Generation, Consistency Check, Exploration (feasibility)

mnemonics.title: Non-Functional Requirement Categories
mnemonics.content: SURPASS: Security, Usability, Reliability, Performance, Availability, Scalability, Supportability

acronyms.usage: Index cards used in object-oriented analysis to identify classes and their relationships during early requirements modeling
Graphical representation showing how data moves through a system, including processes, data stores, external entities, and data flows
Standardized modeling language for visualizing, specifying, constructing, and documenting software system artifacts
Quality attributes specifying system constraints like performance, security, usability, and reliability
Data modeling technique showing entities, attributes, and relationships in a system
Methodology combining analysis and design phases using objects, classes, inheritance, and encapsulation

visualTips.visualization: Think of Aggregation as a 'weak has-a' (Student in a Course - can exist independently). Composition is 'strong has-a' (Pages in Book - cannot exist without Book). Draw aggregation as hollow diamond, composition as filled diamond.

visualTips.concept: Use Case Basic vs Alternative Flow
visualTips.visualization: Basic Flow is the 'happy path' straight line (like main road). Alternative flows are 'detours' branching off when exceptions occur. Draw Basic Flow as main arrow, Alternative Flows as curved branches.
Context Diagram (Level 0) = single process representing whole system. Level 1 = major processes decomposed. Level 2+ = further decomposition. Think of zooming in like Google Maps.
Transitions show what causes state changes. Event[Guard]/Action format: 'Login[credentials valid]/grant access'. Guard is in brackets, action after /.
Cross-reference table with Requirements in rows and Artifacts (Design, Code, Tests) in columns. Mark with 'X' or ID where linked. Empty cells = gap in coverage.

analogies.analogy: Just as an architect's blueprint represents the planned building before construction, the requirements model represents the planned software system before coding. Both capture structure, behavior, and constraints without being the actual product.
Use cases are like early draft user manuals - they describe how users will interact with the system from their perspective, focusing on goals rather than implementation details.
A class diagram is like a DNA map of the system - it defines the fundamental building blocks (genes) and how they relate (genetic connections) that will determine all system behavior.
Multiplicity is like marriage laws: 1..1 = monogamy, 1..* = one spouse can have many children, *..* = dating apps where anyone can connect with anyone.
Validation is like asking 'Are we building the right product?' (aiming at the target). Verification is like asking 'Are we building the product right?' (shooting correctly). Both needed for quality.
